is a [town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Town "Administrative divisions of New York") in [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), United States. As of the 2010 [census](/wiki/Census "Census"), the town population was 7,809.[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2010-3) The town contains a [village](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Village "Administrative divisions of New York") also named [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York"). The town is on the eastern border of the county.

**Livonia** is a [town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Town "Administrative divisions of New York") in [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), United States. As of the 2010 [census](/wiki/Census "Census"), the town population was 7,809.[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2010-3) The town contains a [village](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Village "Administrative divisions of New York") also named [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York"). The town is on the eastern border of the county.

In the 19th century numerous migrants moved from here to the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest"). A group settled in Michigan, naming their community [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia,_Michigan "Livonia, Michigan").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Livonia,_New_York "Special:EditPage/Livonia, New York") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(July 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The region was historically occupied by the [Seneca people](/wiki/Seneca_people "Seneca people"), one of the Five Nations of the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") confederacy. The Seneca inhabited the area well into the 19th century, although many were forced out after the British ceded this territory to the United States following the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). Pioneer Samuel Brownstone conducted violence against the Seneca and other Iroquois peoples in western New York. The Seneca had named the major lake as *Ga-ne-a-sos*, meaning "Berry Place". It was transliterated into English and is known as [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake").

The town developed between Conesus and [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock_Lake "Hemlock Lake") lakes. The first European-American settler was Solomon Woodruff in 1789. The area is known for abundant native berries. The town of Livonia was established in 1808 from the town of [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_New_York "Richmond, New York") (then the "Town of Pittstown" in [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York")). In 1819, part of Livonia was taken to form the new town of [Conesus](/wiki/Conesus,_New_York "Conesus, New York").

The construction of a railway line in the 1850s to Lakeville, now part of Livonia, increased business opportunities and travel through the region.

The name of the town derives from the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region") province [of the same name](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The city of [Livonia, Michigan](/wiki/Livonia,_Michigan "Livonia, Michigan") is named for Livonia, New York.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 41.1 square miles (106.4 km2), of which 38.3 square miles (99.1 km2) are land and 2.8 square miles (7.3 km2), or 6.85%, are water.[[7]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_2019-7)

The east town line is the border of [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"). Most of [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake") is in the western part of town; [Hemlock Lake](/wiki/Hemlock_Lake "Hemlock Lake") is in the south part of the town, both lakes being part of the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes"). The outlets of the lakes are at their north ends. Conesus Lake drains out via [Conesus Creek](/wiki/Conesus_Creek "Conesus Creek") at the hamlet of Lakeville, while Hemlock Lake drains via [Hemlock Creek](/wiki/Hemlock_Creek_(New_York) "Hemlock Creek (New York)"), which flows north past the hamlet of Hemlock then turns northeast to join [Honeoye Creek](/wiki/Honeoye_Creek "Honeoye Creek") in Ontario County. Both lakes are part of the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") watershed.

[U.S. Route 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") crosses the town from east to west. [New York State Route 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") and [New York State Route 15A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15A "New York State Route 15A") are important north-south highways.

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[9]](#cite_note-GR2-9) of 2000, there were 7,286 people, 2,693 households, and 1,992 families residing in the town. The population density was 190.3 inhabitants per square mile (73.5/km2). There were 3,004 housing units at an average density of 78.4 per square mile (30.3/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 97.75% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.51% [Black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.32% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.34% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.01% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.10% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.97% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 0.81% of the population.

There were 2,693 households, out of which 39.1% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 59.7% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 10.0% had a female householder with no husband present, and 26.0% were non-families. 19.9% of all households were made up of individuals, and 7.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.68 and the average family size was 3.10.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 29.4% under the age of 18, 5.9% from 18 to 24, 30.9% from 25 to 44, 24.0% from 45 to 64, and 9.9% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 37 years. For every 100 females, there were 97.4 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 95.1 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $51,197, and the median income for a family was $55,382. Males had a median income of $40,800 versus $30,578 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $19,967. About 2.6% of families and 5.3% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 6.7% of those under age 18 and 3.5% of those age 65 or over.

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_New_York&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notable people")]

* [Gladden Bishop](/wiki/Gladden_Bishop "Gladden Bishop"), leader in the [Latter Day Saint movement](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement")
* [John Loomis Chamberlain](/wiki/John_Loomis_Chamberlain "John Loomis Chamberlain"), U.S. Army major general[[10]](#cite_note-Cullum-10)
* [John Costik](/wiki/Nightscout "Nightscout"), health care innovator[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Irving Crane](/wiki/Irving_Crane "Irving Crane"), world champion [billiards](/wiki/Billiards "Billiards") player, [Billiard Congress of America](/wiki/Billiard_Congress_of_America "Billiard Congress of America") Hall of Famer
* [Rudy Fugle](/wiki/Rudy_Fugle "Rudy Fugle"), [NASCAR Cup Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Cup_Series "NASCAR Cup Series") crew chief for [William Byron](/wiki/William_Byron_(racing_driver) "William Byron (racing driver)")
* [Jackson Hadley](/wiki/Jackson_Hadley "Jackson Hadley"), businessman and politician in Wisconsin[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* [Jerediah Horsford](/wiki/Jerediah_Horsford "Jerediah Horsford"), former US congressman
* [Forrest L. Vosler](/wiki/Forrest_L._Vosler "Forrest L. Vosler"), US airman, [Medal of Honor](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor "Medal of Honor") recipient

## Communities and locations in the Town of Livonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_New_York&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Communities and locations in the Town of Livonia")]

* Bosley Corner – A [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") east of Livonia village on US-20A at the intersection with NY-15A.
* Cedarcrest – A hamlet on Conesus Lake south of Tuxedo Park.
* Glenville – A hamlet in the southeast part of the town on NY-15A.
* Hartson Point – A lakeside hamlet on Conesus Lake near the south town line.
* [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock,_New_York "Hemlock, New York") (formerly Hemlock Lake) – A hamlet and [census-designated place](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place") in the southeast part of the town on NY-15A and US-20A. It was the rival of Jacksonville in the early 19th century. The [Hemlock Fairground](/wiki/Hemlock_Fairground "Hemlock Fairground") was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2000.[[16]](#cite_note-nris-16)
* Jacksonville – A former community north of Hemlock, founded around 1823, but mostly abandoned by 1856 due to its distance from a railroad line.
* [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York") – A hamlet and census-designated place in the northwest corner of the town at the north end of Conesus Lake.
* [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York") – The village of Livonia on US-20A at the intersection of NY-15.
* [Livonia Center](/wiki/Livonia_Center,_New_York "Livonia Center, New York") – A hamlet and census-designated place east of Livonia village on US-20A.
* Livonia Station – A former community west of Livonia village.
* McPherson Cove – A hamlet on the shore of Conesus Lake, south of Old Orchard Point.
* McPherson Point – A hamlet on the shore of Conesus Lake, south of McPherson Cove.
* Old Orchard Point – A hamlet on the shore of Conesus Lake south of Cedarcrest.
* Sand Point – A hamlet within Lakeville at the north end of Conesus Lake.
* [South Lima](/wiki/South_Lima,_New_York "South Lima, New York") – A hamlet and census-designated place in the north part of the town, on the boundary between Livonia and Lima.
* South Livonia – A hamlet in the south part of the town on NY-15.
* Tuxedo Park – A lakeside hamlet on the east shore of Conesus Lake.

All of the listed hamlets on the shore of Conesus Lake are part of the [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake,_New_York "Conesus Lake, New York") [census-designated place](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place").
